author: jsign
content: Opening thread for any questions/comments.

author: taulepton_
content: Nice work! Out of interest, what bugs/edge cases have you found so far?

author: taulepton_
content: Does this framework support differentiating between code that is created during a block and code that was read from state? I think some clients include code that was created in the current block in their witness and this is pretty wasteful. I think we don't need it if we have the tx that created the contract.

author: jsign
content:
> Nice work! Out of interest, what bugs/edge cases have you found so far?
Reg things found in the execution wintess creation spec, we found mainly ways to make the witness a bit more efficient in quite border cases like: if a contract is successfuly created for a new account A, and a later tx executes a CALL-like variant with a matching `code_hash_1`, then technically we can avoid `bytecode` inclusion in the witness. (i.e. the bytecode is the same for both accounts, so there is a nit extra optimization).

The other main find found was mentioned in this release reg the guest program not respecting the executoin witness generation way of calculating hte post state root. If both the execution witness and guest program validating it doesn't respect hte same rules, then it might be the case the guest program can fail to calculate the post state root due to missing sibiling nodes required during branch compressions.

author: jsign
content:
> Does this framework support differentiating between code that is created during a block and code that was read from state? I think some clients include code that was created in the current block in their witness and this is pretty wasteful. I think we don't need it if we have the tx that created the contract.
Yep, exactly. There are already tests covering all these cases to make sure bytecode created during test creation must not appear in the execution witness. This is something that Reth was doing which shouldn't be happening. (And other cases like adding empty bytecodes, and similar nits)

author: jsign
content: `bytecode` and  `headers` fields in the execution witness resulted in 117 tests to have decent coverage -- a priori those fields feel simple, but there are many things to cover reg EXTOCODESIZE/EXTCODECOPY, 7702, and test creation situations with/without reverts etc. I think we have decent coverage now, but we should still double check some very particular revert cases reg OOG and stack overflows.

author: taulepton_
content: Interesting! It's great to have some coverage on these discrepancies. How are you thinking about formalising this? Do you plan to consolidate these findings and implement more explicit specs, or will these tests and the framework serve as the source of truth?

author: jsign
content: Yep, the spec implementation and the implemented tests should be considered the source of truth.

author: jsign
content: Any EL running all these tests should go through all the situations and realize if their exeuction witness has a mismatch

author: taulepton_
content: That makes sense, so optimising the specs may have the effect of optimising the clients

author: jsign
content: Yes (although I would change "may" with "must" 😅 )

author: taulepton_
content: For example, when I was exploring this domain, I saw an inconsistency in clients. Some clients would do a sload and read from disk, and then only check sufficient gas after the load. If we eagerly evaluate gas before doing the storage lookup, then this would optimise witness size and potentially performance.

author: jsign
content: Yes, these cases should all be covered. On this front BAL already forced them to be coherent here, because now the state access happening before or after some points is observable.

author: jsign
content: And the BAL must be canonical.

author: taulepton_
content: yep, makes sense!

author: taulepton_
content: do you have any stats on witness size imporvements following these changes?

author: jsign
content: Not really -- usually having historical execution witness and re-running them is quite challenging for comparisons, since re-executing old blocks have some probs reg state rewinding, so feels somewhat challenging (at lesat in the Reth case where I found many execution witness inefficiencies)

author: jsign
content: I chatted with Ivan from Ethrex, and after they pass all the execution witness tests, we are planning to try doing some full sync from one year ago until the tip so we have some cannonical/spec mainnet execution witness

author: jsign
content: Considering now those execution witness are canonical,, this dataset will be quite useful to know more about sizes or give to all guest programs to try/benchmark/test/etc.

author: taulepton_
content: yeah that would be great. I suppose this could also serve as a base case for benchmarking new state tree models (at least from a witness-size perspective).
